draft.introduction: The Taittiriya Upanishad’s Brahmananda Valli maps reality in concentric sheaths (koshas): food, breath, mind, knowledge, and bliss. The innermost layer is Brahman itself.

translation.text: From Brahman, which is the Self, was produced space. From space, air. From air, fire. From fire, water. From water, earth. From earth, plants. From plants, food. From food, the person.
translation.original_text: brahmaṇo vā etad ākāśaḥ sambhūtaḥ | ākāśād vāyuḥ | vāyor agniḥ

translation.text: He who knows Brahman attains the highest. Brahman is truth, knowledge, and infinity. He who knows it as hidden in the cave of the heart and in the highest heaven realizes all desires together with the all-knowing Brahman.
translation.original_text: brahma vid āpnoti param | tad eṣā ’bhyuktā | satyaṃ jñānam anantaṃ brahma

translator_notes.note: The five-sheath (pañca-kośa) model influenced later Vedantic psychology profoundly. Śaṅkara reads the sheaths as progressive negations: you are not the body, not the breath, not the mind. What remains when all sheaths are stripped away is Brahman.
